name: childcare-assistance
description: Subsidized childcare programs, Head Start eligibility, emergency childcare options, and childcare tax benefits. The barrier that keeps parents from working or studying.
source: Liberation Labs mutual aid knowledge base

triples.subject: CCDF (Child Care Development Fund)
triples.object: childcare costs for parents who are working, in job training, or attending school — income limits vary by state

triples.subject: Head Start
triples.object: children ages 3-5 from families below the federal poverty line — free preschool plus meals, health screenings, and family services

triples.subject: Early Head Start
triples.object: pregnant women and children birth to age 3 — home-based or center-based options

triples.subject: approved training program
triples.object: childcare subsidy in most states — being enrolled in education/training counts as an eligible activity

triples.subject: childcare subsidy
triples.object: apply through local Department of Social Services or childcare resource and referral agency — waitlists are common

triples.subject: Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit
triples.object: tax credit of 20-35% of childcare expenses up to $3,000 per child ($6,000 for two or more)

triples.subject: Dependent Care FSA
triples.object: pre-tax dollars (up to $5,000/year) for childcare expenses through employer benefits plan

triples.subject: emergency childcare voucher
triples.object: 211 referral, local community action agency, or employer assistance programs

triples.subject: childcare resource and referral (CCR&R)
triples.object: find licensed providers, understand subsidy options, and navigate the application process — every state has one

triples.subject: family child care
triples.object: more affordable and flexible than center-based care — licensed providers who care for children in their homes

triples.subject: school-age care
triples.object: before/after school programs, Boys & Girls Clubs, YMCA, and parks department programs — often sliding scale

triples.subject: summer programs
triples.object: camps, library programs, parks department activities — many offer free or reduced-rate options for low-income families

triples.subject: parent co-op
triples.object: shared childcare among families who take turns — reduces cost to zero but requires schedule coordination

triples.subject: childcare waitlist
triples.object: apply to multiple programs simultaneously, ask about cancellation lists, and reapply if initially denied — persistence matters

triples.subject: military childcare
triples.object: active duty, Guard/Reserve, and eligible DOD civilians — Child Development Centers operate on a sliding fee scale

triples.subject: tribal childcare
triples.object: CCDF Tribal Set-Aside — federally recognized tribes administer their own childcare assistance programs

triples.subject: special needs childcare
triples.object: IDEA Part C (birth-3) and Part B (3-21) provide services including therapeutic childcare at no cost to families

triples.subject: kinship care
triples.object: kinship navigator programs, TANF child-only grants, and foster care subsidies when grandparents or relatives are primary caregivers

triples.subject: childcare provider quality
triples.object: state licensing databases, QRIS (Quality Rating and Improvement System) star ratings, and parent reviews

triples.subject: sick child care
triples.object: mildly-ill childcare programs (some centers offer this), employer-provided backup care, and family medical leave (FMLA) for caregiver absences
